items.correct: Increases red blood cell production, improving oxygen-carrying capacity.
items.why: Students assume all PEDs work the same way. EPO is specifically for endurance - more red cells = more oxygen to working muscles. Anabolic steroids do something different (promote protein synthesis).

items.q: According to the inverted-U hypothesis, when is performance best?
items.correct: At an optimal moderate level of arousal.
items.why: Students assume 'more psyched up = better'. The inverted-U shows performance rises with arousal up to an optimum, then DECLINES with excess arousal (the athlete chokes). The optimum varies by skill.

items.correct: Volume of blood pumped by the heart per minute (stroke volume x heart rate).
items.misconception: The volume of blood in the heart at any moment.
items.why: Students confuse stroke volume (one beat) with cardiac output (per minute total). CO = SV x HR. A 70mL stroke at 70bpm = 4.9L/min cardiac output.

items.q: How many cervical vertebrae are there?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 7. “12” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Bone adaptation to load. “Lung volumes” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Total vertebrae in the human spine?
items.why: The real figure is 33 vertebrae. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
